Lesson Title: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
Description: 
Type: blocks

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

Researchers and practitioners have been studying writing instruction for many decades to identify effective instructional practices. Why is it important for teachers to become familiar with this research?

Teachers who deliver high quality instruction combine what is sometimes referred to as the art of teaching (how they teach in an engaging way) with the science of teaching (what research tells us is most effective instruction). It is essential for teachers to be sure their instruction is supported by research - this is often referred to as evidence-based instruction.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Heading: Heading Paragraph:

While the goal for this Keys to Early Writing professional development course is to provide practical teaching suggestions, references and connections to research findings that support these suggestions are embedded throughout the course. You are encouraged to become familiar with writing research so you can explain to colleagues and administrators why you are using what you learn in the course or to support practices you already use.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

General Writing Research Findings

Paragraph:

There are several teaching practices that come up as effective across numerous studies, listed below.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

  • Teach students that there are different purposes for writing.

  • Assign writing tasks for real audiences that are relevant to students’ everyday lives.

  • Teach the stages of the writing process.

  • Explicitly teach writing skills, strategies, and techniques.

  • Provide procedural scaffolds.

Paragraph:

  • Provide opportunities for students to collaborate with peers and the teacher.

  • Use mentor models for students to emulate.

  • Provide feedback.

  • Increase the amount students write and time spent on writing instruction.

  • Create a supportive, engaging writing environment.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

Sources: Hidi & Boscolo, 2006; Chapman, 2006; Donovan & Smolkin, 2006; Boscolo & Gelati, 2007; Coker, 2007; MacArthur, 2007; Pritchard & Honeycutt, 2007

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Writing Research Findings Related to Early Writing Instruction

Paragraph:

There are two research sources based on meta-analyses of research on effective elementary writing instruction that are referenced throughout the course. The first is Teaching Elementary School Students to Be Effective Writers (Graham et al., 2012) that offers four main recommendations:

  1. Provide daily time for students to write. 
  2. Teach students to use the writing process for a variety of purposes.
  3. Teach students to become fluent with handwriting, spelling, sentence construction, typing, and word processing. 
  4. Create an engaged community of writers. 

Review the details for these recommendations in the document below. You can download a copy for your records. Watch the video that summarizes the recommendations, and then view the infographic after the video (Institute of Education Sciences).

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Research Meta-Analysis Article 

Paragraph:

The second research source based on a meta-analysis of research related to grades kindergarten through grade 8 is the article Research-Based Writing Practices and the Common Core: Meta-Analysis and Meta-Synthesis (Graham, Harris, Santangelo, 2015).  Review the article's 13 recommendations (with explanations for each recommendation) in the document below. When you are done, select the boxes next to any practices in the list below that you use on a regular basis. Be sure to keep these evidence-based practices in mind as you teach writing!

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

1. Create a writing environment that is positive and supportive.

Paragraph:

2. Establish writing routines that create a pleasant and motivating writing environment.

Paragraph:

3. Implement a process approach to writing.

Paragraph:

4. Create routines that ensure students write frequently.

Paragraph:

5. Design instructional routines where students compose together.

Paragraph:

6. Establish goals for students' writing.

Paragraph:

7. Use twenty-first-century writing tools.

Paragraph:

8. Provide feedback.

Paragraph:

9. Ensure students acquire needed writing skills, knowledge, and strategies.

Paragraph:

10. Teach foundational writing skills.

Paragraph:

11. Increase students' knowledge about writing.

Paragraph:

12. Teach students strategies for planning, drafting, revising, and editing.

Paragraph:

13. Use writing as a tool to support students' learning.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Response Notebook: (Required) Reflect and Apply

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

After reviewing the descriptions of the 13 Research Recommendations, select 2 ideas and describe how you would integrate them into your writing instruction.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Supplemental Resources

Paragraph:
  • Research Guide:  Teaching Elementary School Students to Be Effective Writers (Graham et al., 2012, Institute of Education Sciences)
  • Research Article: Research-Based Writing Practices and the Common Core: Meta-analysis and Meta-synthesis (Graham, Harris, Santangelo, 2015, The Elementary School Journal)

pdf_content.title: PDF: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction - PDF Content: Research Based Writing Practices (173KB)
pdf_content.content:
--- Page 1 ---

Figure 1.6 
Research -Based  Writing  Practices  and the Common  Core:  
Meta -analysis and Meta -synthesis 
(Graham,  Harris,  Santangelo,  2015)  
 
This article summarizes research -supported practices that can be used to meet Common Core 
State  Standards  writing  objectives  in kindergarten  to grade  eight.  These  practices  were  identified 
by conducting a meta -analysis of writing intervention studies and the practices of exceptional 
literacy teachers.  
 
Recommendations  
1. Create  a Writing  Environment  That  is Positive  and Supportive  
 
Given  the demands  of state  writing  standards,  it is important  to develop  a writing  environment 
that is motivating, pleasant, and nonthreatening, where teachers support students and their 
writing efforts, and students support each other.  
 
2. Establish  Writing  Routines  That  Create  a Pleasant  and Motivating  Writing  Environment  
 
It is helpful  to establish  routines  and create  a positive  classroom  where  students  share  their  work 
with others, are encouraged to try hard, and are motivated to write.  
 
3. Implement  a Process  Approach  to Writing  
 
A process approach to writing includes creating a pleasant and positive writing environment, 
promoting high levels of student interactions to support writing, writing for real audiences and 
authentic purposes, stressing personal responsibility and ownership of writing projects, and 
encouraging  self-reflection  and evaluation.  This type  of model  was found  to have  a positive  effect 
on the quality of writing produced by students in grades 1 to 8.  
4. Create  Routines  That  Ensure  Students  Write  Frequently  
 
The basic premise is that students must write frequently if they are to grow as writers. Research 
finds that when teachers increased how frequently students in grades 2 -8 wrote, there was a 
corresponding  improvement  in the quality  of their  writing.  It also finds  that for students  in grades 
2-6, extra writing made students better readers.  
 
5. Design  Instructional  Routines  Where  Students  Compose  Together  
 
Having students work together when writing is an effective practice. A common characteristic of 
studies  that tested  collaborative  writing  was that students  were  provided  with  specific  directions 
for what to do when working together and then taught how to carry out these guidelines.

--- Page 2 ---

6. Establish  Goals  for Students’  Writing  
 
Research  finds  that writing  quality  is enhanced  when  teachers  set high,  but realistic  expectations 
for students’ writing. This includes setting clear, specific, and reasonably challenging goals for 
student writing.  
 
7. Use Twenty -First -Century  Writing  Tools  
 
Word processors have a number of advantages over writing by hand, as electronic text is legible, 
and it can easily  be deleted,  added,  rewritten,  or moved.  Research  finds  that the quality  of writing 
of students in grades 1 to 8 who used word processing versus writing by hand improved.  
 
8. Provide  Feedback  
 
Research finds that the use of formative assessment, (teacher, peer, and self -assessment) 
improved children’s writing. When teachers monitor students’ progress as writers they can  
adjust  classroom  practices  to meet  the collective  as well as the individual needs  of their  students.  
 
9. Ensure  Students  Acquire  Needed  Writing  Skills,  Knowledge,  and Strategies  
 
Research supports teaching writing skills (e.g., handwriting, spelling, grammar and usage), 
attributes  of specific  types  of text (e.g.,  opinion,  informative,  narrative)  and writing  strategies  that 
support planning, revising and editing.  
 
10. Teach  Foundational  Writing  Skills  
 
Research finds that teaching spelling and handwriting to grade 1 to 3 students enhanced the 
overall  quality  of their  writing.  It also finds  that teaching  sentence  construction  through  sentence - 
combining improves the quality of student writing.  
11. Increase  Students’  Knowledge  About  Writing  
 
Three  types  of knowledge  that are important  are knowledge  about  the writing  topic,  knowledge 
about genre, and knowledge about the words used to convey meaning. Research supports the 
following:  
• Have students gather ideas and information to write about through prewriting activities 
such  as locating  writing  content  through  brainstorming,  reading,  drawing,  and/or  by using 
graphic organizers.  
• Teach  students  the basic  elements  of the different  types  of text (text  structure).  
• Provide  students  with  good  models  of written  text.  
• Teach  students  vocabulary  that will improve  their  text.  
 
12. Teach  Students  Strategies  for Planning,  Drafting,  Revising,  and Editing

--- Page 3 ---

Research  finds  that it is beneficial  for teachers  to encourage  students  to employ  a variety  of 
planning, revising, and editing strategies to help them create and improve what they write. 
Research also supports the explicit teaching of strategies.  
 
13. Use Writing  as a Tool  to Support  Students’  Learning  
 
Research  supports  having  students  use writing  as a tool for learning,  including  writing  about 
content presented in class and writing about materials read. This results in improved 
comprehension of such material. Writing to learn activities include writing short answers to 
questions, taking notes, writing a summary, and writing more extended responses.

9. Ensure  Students  Acquire  Needed  Writing  Skills,  Knowledge,  and Strategies  
 
Research supports teaching writing skills (e.g., handwriting, spelling, grammar and usage), 
attributes  of specific  types  of text (e.g.,  opinion,  informative,  narrative)  and writing  strategies  that 
support planning, revising and editing.  
 
10. Teach  Foundational  Writing  Skills  
 
Research finds that teaching spelling and handwriting to grade 1 to 3 students enhanced the 
overall  quality  of their  writing.  It also finds  that teaching  sentence  construction  through  sentence - 
combining improves the quality of student writing.  
11. Increase  Students’  Knowledge  About  Writing  
 
Three  types  of knowledge  that are important  are knowledge  about  the writing  topic,  knowledge 
about genre, and knowledge about the words used to convey meaning. Research supports the 
following:  
• Have students gather ideas and information to write about through prewriting activities 
such  as locating  writing  content  through  brainstorming,  reading,  drawing,  and/or  by using 
graphic organizers.  
• Teach  students  the basic  elements  of the different  types  of text (text  structure).  
• Provide  students  with  good  models  of written  text.  
• Teach  students  vocabulary  that will improve  their  text.  
 
12. Teach  Students  Strategies  for Planning,  Drafting,  Revising,  and Editing

--- Page 3 ---

Research  finds  that it is beneficial  for teachers  to encourage  students  to employ  a variety  of 
planning, revising, and editing strategies to help them create and improve what they write. 
Research also supports the explicit teaching of strategies.  
 
13. Use Writing  as a Tool  to Support  Students’  Learning  
 
Research  supports  having  students  use writing  as a tool for learning,  including  writing  about 
content presented in class and writing about materials read. This results in improved 
comprehension of such material. Writing to learn activities include writing short answers to 
questions, taking notes, writing a summary, and writing more extended responses.

paragraphs: Figure 1.6
Research -Based  Writing  Practices  and the Common  Core:
Meta -analysis and Meta -synthesis
(Graham,  Harris,  Santangelo,  2015)
This article summarizes research -supported practices that can be used to meet Common Core
State  Standards  writing  objectives  in kindergarten  to grade  eight.  These  practices  were  identified
by conducting a meta -analysis of writing intervention studies and the practices of exceptional
literacy teachers.
Recommendations
1. Create  a Writing  Environment  That  is Positive  and Supportive
Given  the demands  of state  writing  standards,  it is important  to develop  a writing  environment
that is motivating, pleasant, and nonthreatening, where teachers support students and their
writing efforts, and students support each other.
2. Establish  Writing  Routines  That  Create  a Pleasant  and Motivating  Writing  Environment
It is helpful  to establish  routines  and create  a positive  classroom  where  students  share  their  work
with others, are encouraged to try hard, and are motivated to write.
3. Implement  a Process  Approach  to Writing
A process approach to writing includes creating a pleasant and positive writing environment,
promoting high levels of student interactions to support writing, writing for real audiences and
authentic purposes, stressing personal responsibility and ownership of writing projects, and
encouraging  self-reflection  and evaluation.  This type  of model  was found  to have  a positive  effect
on the quality of writing produced by students in grades 1 to 8.
4. Create  Routines  That  Ensure  Students  Write  Frequently
The basic premise is that students must write frequently if they are to grow as writers. Research
finds that when teachers increased how frequently students in grades 2 -8 wrote, there was a
corresponding  improvement  in the quality  of their  writing.  It also finds  that for students  in grades
2-6, extra writing made students better readers.
5. Design  Instructional  Routines  Where  Students  Compose  Together
Having students work together when writing is an effective practice. A common characteristic of
studies  that tested  collaborative  writing  was that students  were  provided  with  specific  directions
for what to do when working together and then taught how to carry out these guidelines.

Figure 1.6 
Research -Based  Writing  Practices  and the Common  Core:  
Meta -analysis and Meta -synthesis 
(Graham,  Harris,  Santangelo,  2015)  
 
This article summarizes research -supported practices that can be used to meet Common Core 
State  Standards  writing  objectives  in kindergarten  to grade  eight.  These  practices  were  identified 
by conducting a meta -analysis of writing intervention studies and the practices of exceptional 
literacy teachers.  
 
Recommendations  
1. Create  a Writing  Environment  That  is Positive  and Supportive  
 
Given  the demands  of state  writing  standards,  it is important  to develop  a writing  environment 
that is motivating, pleasant, and nonthreatening, where teachers support students and their 
writing efforts, and students support each other.  
 
2. Establish  Writing  Routines  That  Create  a Pleasant  and Motivating  Writing  Environment  
 
It is helpful  to establish  routines  and create  a positive  classroom  where  students  share  their  work 
with others, are encouraged to try hard, and are motivated to write.

4. Create  Routines  That  Ensure  Students  Write  Frequently  
 
The basic premise is that students must write frequently if they are to grow as writers. Research

paragraphs: 6. Establish  Goals  for Students’  Writing
Research  finds  that writing  quality  is enhanced  when  teachers  set high,  but realistic  expectations
for students’ writing. This includes setting clear, specific, and reasonably challenging goals for
student writing.
7. Use Twenty -First -Century  Writing  Tools
Word processors have a number of advantages over writing by hand, as electronic text is legible,
and it can easily  be deleted,  added,  rewritten,  or moved.  Research  finds  that the quality  of writing
of students in grades 1 to 8 who used word processing versus writing by hand improved.
8. Provide  Feedback
Research finds that the use of formative assessment, (teacher, peer, and self -assessment)
improved children’s writing. When teachers monitor students’ progress as writers they can
adjust  classroom  practices  to meet  the collective  as well as the individual needs  of their  students.
9. Ensure  Students  Acquire  Needed  Writing  Skills,  Knowledge,  and Strategies
Research supports teaching writing skills (e.g., handwriting, spelling, grammar and usage),
attributes  of specific  types  of text (e.g.,  opinion,  informative,  narrative)  and writing  strategies  that
support planning, revising and editing.
10. Teach  Foundational  Writing  Skills
Research finds that teaching spelling and handwriting to grade 1 to 3 students enhanced the
overall  quality  of their  writing.  It also finds  that teaching  sentence  construction  through  sentence -
combining improves the quality of student writing.
11. Increase  Students’  Knowledge  About  Writing
Three  types  of knowledge  that are important  are knowledge  about  the writing  topic,  knowledge
about genre, and knowledge about the words used to convey meaning. Research supports the
following:
• Have students gather ideas and information to write about through prewriting activities
such  as locating  writing  content  through  brainstorming,  reading,  drawing,  and/or  by using
graphic organizers.
• Teach  students  the basic  elements  of the different  types  of text (text  structure).
• Provide  students  with  good  models  of written  text.
• Teach  students  vocabulary  that will improve  their  text.
12. Teach  Students  Strategies  for Planning,  Drafting,  Revising,  and Editing

transcript_content.title: Transcript: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction - Video Transcript: Handwriting
transcript_content.content: Let's talk about handwriting and Oregon keyboarding if you want to get something out on paper you have to be able to write it you also have to be able to spell it but even if you know how to spell the word you have to be able to get those letters down on the paper and if students of any age have to put too much energy into thinking about how to form those letters it's going to take energy away from the critical thinking parts of being able to write now there's a lot of research out there that shows that students by the time they're in 4th grade if they're not fluent with that handwriting and spending too much it really effects the quality of their writing of their composing now there's uncertainty there's no set answer to the question about should it be cursive should it be manuscript but we do know that whichever it is students have to become fluent in that now let's also make the connection to keyboarding or typing very young children don't have the they're not developmentally able to do the kind of you know reporting that adults and older students can but they can hunt and Peck once students hit about 5th or 6th grade they're developmentally more able to do the keyboarding and there's great debate over whether we should spend time with handwriting or go right to keyboarding you're not here to answer that question I do believe that for elementary children in particular there's also so much research that shows that if students are learning to handwrite the letters at the same time that they're learning the letter sound correspondences that improves the letter writing and it also improves the alphabetic knowledge that they're developping during a phonics lesson so for lots of reasons the ability to be fluent in that transcription skill of handwriting and or keyboarding is absolutely essential
